one_liner: Five days of fatherhood is not enough.
search_doc:
Five days of fatherhood is not enough.
Mechanisms: legislative-or-policy-shift, industry-practice-change, stereotype-flipped-defensibly, purpose-led-holiday-hijack
Idea: On Father's Day, O Boticário decided to sell a cause rather than products. They launched a heartwarming film featuring a father spending his first 5 days with his baby. When it comes to the 6th day, it abruptly cuts to the father working at an office. They also created a supply to help companies, HR departments and entrepreneurs adopt extended parental leave.
Insight: Five days of paternity leave isn't a 'break' — it's an institutionalised signal that childcare is still mum's job, and that needs to be challenged from inside the marketing of Father's Day itself.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/BRONZE/5 days_ Give Me A Break - O Boticário (AlmapBBDO, Sao Paulo).jpeg

extracted.tagline: A Father's Day campaign that was a gift to fathers, companies, and society as a whole.
extracted.context: In Brazil, maternity leave is typically 120 days, while paternity leave is often limited to only 5 days. O Boticário, Brazil's largest beauty brand, is committed to gender equality. The company offers 120 days of paid leave for both mothers and fathers. That's why they launched a Father's Day campaign aimed at grabbing media attention and closing this gap.
extracted.idea: On Father's Day, O Boticário decided to sell a cause rather than products. They launched a heartwarming film featuring a father spending his first 5 days with his baby. When it comes to the 6th day, it abruptly cuts to the father working at an office. They also created a supply to help companies, HR departments and entrepreneurs adopt extended parental leave.
execution: Father's Day film showing the first 5 days of paternity then cutting to the father back at the office
End-of-film lettering: 'Let's talk about parental leaving?' inviting all society to debate the subject
Open-source supply / toolkit for companies and HR departments to adopt extended parental leave
Partner brand endorsements (Mastercard, Amazon Brasil, Diageo Brasil, Volvo Car Latin America, Nubank)
metrics: 6 draft bills at the Brazilian Congress
+200 companies using the guide to implement 120 days as a policy
Best Recall — Father's Day campaign among beauty brands

inferred.insight: Five days of paternity leave isn't a 'break' — it's an institutionalised signal that childcare is still mum's job, and that needs to be challenged from inside the marketing of Father's Day itself.
inferred.one_liner: Five days of fatherhood is not enough.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns Father's Day into a parental-leave reform platform: 516M impressions, 6 draft bills tabled in the Brazilian Congress, and 200+ companies adopting 120-day shared leave as a policy.

visual.style_description: Cinematic film-still hero of two close-ups of a young bearded father, one cradling his baby and one back at a desk, separated by the bright green 'FIVE IS NOT ENOUGH' headline; thumbnail strip of Day 1-6 stills above, press logo band and 516MM / 96MM / 1.8MM metric blocks below, with partner-brand quote cards on the right.

idea.rationale: Hijacking Father's Day — a retail high-point for a beauty brand — to argue against Brazil's 5-day paternity leave is a defensible holiday-subversion, and the cut from 'Day 5 with baby' to 'Day 6 at the office' is a clean filmic device. The idea is well-formed but sits closer to a familiar 'film + open-source toolkit + policy push' template than to a category-redefining mechanic.

strategy.rationale: O Boticário is Brazil's largest beauty brand and one of the few companies that already offers 120 days of paid leave for both mothers and fathers — that internal policy is what gives the brand authority to argue for legislative reform rather than borrowing the cause from outside. The 120-vs-5 day gap between maternal and paternal leave is a verifiable statutory fact, and the framing of '5 days isn't a break, it's a signal that childcare is mum's job' identifies the stereotype precisely. Fathers themselves are the subject more than co-authors of the work, which is a soft spot for a Glass entry.

execution.rationale: The film is craft-led and the campaign is orchestrated as a coalition rather than a one-shot, with endorsements from Mastercard, Amazon Brasil, Diageo Brasil, Volvo Car Latin America and Nubank acting as multi-brand cover for the policy ask. The open-source HR toolkit gives the campaign a tangible adoption mechanic for companies, and the news push lands across CNN, Forbes, Globo, Folha, Exame and Valor — the right outlets for a corporate-policy debate.

impact.rationale: Six draft bills tabled in the Brazilian Congress and 200+ companies adopting the 120-day shared-leave policy are concrete institutional-change outcomes — the right business outcome for a leave-reform campaign. The 516MM impressions, 96MM reach and 1.8MM online visits are entrant-reported and unanchored ('Best Recall — Father's Day among beauty brands' is also self-reported), so the load-bearing impact is the policy adoption rather than the surface numbers. No law has yet passed and no O Boticário sales / brand-equity lift is shown, which keeps the result short of a Gold-tier behaviour-change story.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because draft bills and 200+ corporate adoptions are real policy-influence outcomes, but the headline reach figures are unverified and the law itself has not yet been enacted.

one_liner: Real wedding invitations, drawn by children.
search_doc:
Real wedding invitations, drawn by children.
Mechanisms: direct-mail-confrontation, victim-as-author, personalisation-of-statistics, legislative-or-policy-shift
Idea: To motivate lawmakers to pass a bill protecting children from child marriage and put an end to this harmful practice, UN Women chose to target leaders with a direct mail campaign. Members of the National Assembly of Pakistan received invitations to fictional children's weddings through wedding cards that would suit a child's wedding, designed entirely by children.
Insight: Lawmakers debate child marriage in abstract statistics — but a handwritten wedding invitation from a 9-year-old makes the issue physical, personal, and impossible to file away.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/BRONZE/Child Wedding Cards - UN Women (Impact BBDO Dubai).jpeg

extracted.tagline: A direct mail campaign aimed at lawmakers to increase the minimum age of marriage to 18.
extracted.context: 21% of girls in Pakistan are married off before the age of 18. According to UNICEF, nearly 19 million are child brides. The UN children's agency estimates that around 4.6 million were married before the age of 15. Some as young as 5. UN Women, the United Nations entity dedicated to gender equality and the empowerment of women, has been actively fighting against the harmful practice of child marriage in Pakistan.
extracted.idea: To motivate lawmakers to pass a bill protecting children from child marriage and put an end to this harmful practice, UN Women chose to target leaders with a direct mail campaign. Members of the National Assembly of Pakistan received invitations to fictional children's weddings through wedding cards that would suit a child's wedding, designed entirely by children.
execution: Hand-drawn wedding invitations created by young girls aged 5 to 15 from socio-economic backgrounds
Each invitation listed a real socio-economic child bride and her details
Cards mailed to members of the National Assembly of Pakistan
Documentation of the assembly's reaction and follow-up press push
press_coverage: Federal Shariah Court announces 18 as the minimum age of marriage.

inferred.insight: Lawmakers debate child marriage in abstract statistics — but a handwritten wedding invitation from a 9-year-old makes the issue physical, personal, and impossible to file away.
inferred.one_liner: Real wedding invitations, drawn by children.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Confronts Pakistan's National Assembly with hand-drawn wedding invitations from real child brides; Pakistan's Federal Shariah Court subsequently announced 18 as the minimum age of marriage.

visual.style_description: Top half is a row of five hand-drawn pastel children's wedding cards held in front of a young girl in white; under it the title 'child wedding cards' rendered in a child's blue handwriting; bottom strip shows reportage photos of girls drawing, the National Assembly and the Federal Shariah Court announcement callout.

idea.rationale: Hand-drawn wedding invitations from real child brides, mailed directly to members of the National Assembly of Pakistan, collapse the statistic of '19 million child brides' into a physical artefact a lawmaker has to hold. Casting the affected children themselves as the designers of the cards is a precise, defensible victim-as-author mechanic that the format earns.

strategy.rationale: UN Women is the UN entity dedicated to gender equality and women's empowerment, and has been actively campaigning against child marriage in Pakistan for years — so direct legislative lobbying is core mission, not borrowed purpose. The cited UNICEF figures (21% of Pakistani girls married before 18, 19 million child brides, 4.6 million before age 15) are sourced to a named agency rather than anonymous research, which is unusually strong insight backing for the category. The girls themselves are positioned as authors of the cards, satisfying the Glass community-as-co-author test.

execution.rationale: The direct-mail mechanic is tightly targeted at the National Assembly members who hold the legislative power, with each card carrying the name and details of a real child bride to make the statistic individually attributable. Beyond the cards themselves, the orchestration is thin — there is no broad public campaign, coalition or sustained press loop on the board, which makes the work feel surgical rather than amplified.

impact.rationale: The board reports a single but materially load-bearing outcome — Pakistan's Federal Shariah Court announcing 18 as the minimum age of marriage — which, if directly attributable, is the strongest possible business outcome for a legislative campaign. However, the board carries no metrics block at all (no recipient response data, no press reach, no public-opinion shift) and a Shariah Court announcement is not the same as a binding parliamentary law, so the causal chain from cards to court ruling is implied rather than evidenced. The impact case is therefore strong in intent but thin in proof.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the idea is on-mission and arrestingly executed and a Federal Shariah Court ruling is cited, but the impact case carries no metrics and the causal link between the cards and the ruling is asserted rather than demonstrated.

one_liner: A Google Maps for safe, clean toilets women can actually use.
search_doc:
A Google Maps for safe, clean toilets women can actually use.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, purpose-driven-product, data-driven-newsmaking-on-inequality, crowdsourced-utility
Idea: Harpic, India's leading toilet cleaner brand, launched the Loocator — an app that helps women find clean toilets nearby, rate them and even add new toilets to the network. Leveraging the Google Maps API with location data, tech and crowdsourcing, the app expanded women with one practical thing: the ability to find a safe restroom and pee with dignity.
Insight: For Indian women, the absence of usable public toilets is not just an inconvenience — it's a daily health risk that is treated as their problem to endure instead of as a basic right.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/SILVER/Loocator - Harpic India (TGTHR Mumbai_Reckitt Benckiser).jpeg

extracted.tagline: Helping women locate their fundamental right to pee. #BeFreeToPee
extracted.context: In India, restrooms for women are everywhere, but they aren't easy to find. Holding back urine for hours is a nightmare that doesn't get over even if they find one. Public toilets in India are often disgustingly filthy, lacking doors, electricity and even running water. So much so that 70% of Indian women can't find a useable public toilet. To avoid them, women hold their pee by braving excruciating pain — in some cases for up to 22 hours at a stretch. 81% of women don't drink water, no matter the weather. This often leads to complications like UTIs, kidney infection, head strokes and long-term bladder and kidney problems.
extracted.idea: Harpic, India's leading toilet cleaner brand, launched the Loocator — an app that helps women find clean toilets nearby, rate them and even add new toilets to the network. Leveraging the Google Maps API with location data, tech and crowdsourcing, the app expanded women with one practical thing: the ability to find a safe restroom and pee with dignity.
execution: Loocator app integrating Google Maps API with location data
In-app rating system so women can vouch for clean toilets
Crowdsourced additions of new toilet locations
Endorsements from renowned personalities to amplify support
Public communications under the #BeFreeToPee hashtag
metrics: 27% increase in Google Trends within 24 hours

inferred.insight: For Indian women, the absence of usable public toilets is not just an inconvenience — it's a daily health risk that is treated as their problem to endure instead of as a basic right.
inferred.one_liner: A Google Maps for safe, clean toilets women can actually use.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns a toilet-cleaner brand into an infrastructure layer for women's right to pee — 64K+ downloads, 163M+ reach and over $1M in earned media, putting a hidden health crisis on India's national agenda.

visual.style_description: Half-width portrait of a smiling Indian woman in office wear looking at her phone against a deep navy wall, with the chunky red Harpic 'LOOCATOR' logotype top-left and a vertical strip of phone-screen mockups of the app interface on the right; metric blocks and press logos run down the lower right column.

idea.rationale: Loocator turns a toilet-cleaner brand into a women-only mapping utility for safe, clean public toilets — a useful product rather than a campaign. The #BeFreeToPee framing names a specific, under-discussed inequality (right to urinate in public space) and the Google Maps jailbreak is a logical, defensible mechanic for the category.

strategy.rationale: Harpic is Reckitt Benckiser's leading toilet-cleaner brand in India whose 'Mission Swachhta aur Paani' / clean-toilets platform has anchored its category-purpose work for years, so building a women's restroom-finder is direct mission-fit, not borrowed feminism. The cited statistics — 70% of Indian women can't find a usable public toilet, 81% don't drink water to avoid them, holding urine up to 22 hours — are striking but the board does not name the studies behind them, leaving the insight plausible-but-unsourced. The affected community shows up as user, not co-author, which is a real Glass-axis soft spot.

execution.rationale: The app combines Google Maps API, location data and crowdsourced ratings so women can both find and contribute clean toilets, plus a rating system that vouches for venues — a working two-sided utility rather than a one-off launch. Celebrity endorsements and #BeFreeToPee social amplification extend the reach, but the orchestration leans on press and influencer push rather than retailer, NGO or government partnerships that would have given the network durability.

impact.rationale: 64K+ downloads is a concrete adoption number with an absolute base, which is the right business outcome for a utility app. The 163M+ reach and $1.03M earned media are entrant-reported claims rather than verified, and the 27% Google Trends lift in 24 hours is anchored to a single keyword window rather than sustained category interest. The board does not show whether new toilets were actually added to the network at scale, or whether any policy / municipal partnership followed — which is the durability test for a Glass infrastructure idea.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the Google Maps jailbreak for women's right to pee is on-brief and on-mission for Harpic, but the impact case rests on adoption and reach claims rather than documented network growth or policy change.

one_liner: The first stock index that prices the bias against women leaders.
search_doc:
The first stock index that prices the bias against women leaders.
Mechanisms: data-as-content, media-as-product, legislative-or-policy-shift, industry-practice-change, stereotype-flipped-defensibly
Idea: Pink Chip is a live index that makes the success of female leadership impossible to ignore by reframing it as a financial one. It tracks the performance of female-led businesses and measures them against industry benchmarks, contributing to UN Women's Sustainable Development Goal 5.5.
Insight: Investors and the wider market only act on financial proof — so the most persuasive way to argue for female leadership is to put a stock-market price on the bias against it.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/GOLD/Pink Chip - Degiro, Un Women (AKQA Amsterdam).jpeg

extracted.tagline: The first index to put a price on bias.
extracted.context: Research shows that female leaders are better for business but the market is biased against them. Today, only 7% of CEOs of publicly listed companies around the world are women. When they're appointed, biased investors sell their shares and cause stock prices to fall by 2-3%. This bias isn't just bad for society, it's a poor investment strategy.
extracted.idea: Pink Chip is a live index that makes the success of female leadership impossible to ignore by reframing it as a financial one. It tracks the performance of female-led businesses and measures them against industry benchmarks, contributing to UN Women's Sustainable Development Goal 5.5.
execution: AI and name-recognition software trawled corporate filings to build a first-ever data-set of women-led companies
Created Pink Chip U.S., the first live index of women-led businesses
Freely available on PinkChip.org and on the DEGIRO platform so investors can compare and invest directly in women-led businesses
Listed on Bloomberg under PINKCHIP:IND
Advisory board of academics established across five countries
metrics: +80% outperformance of market benchmarks over 4 years
14.5k new investors in Pink Chip companies in the EU in the first three weeks
6.7% stock price increase in Pink Chip companies
9.48% collective rise of the Pink Chip index

inferred.insight: Investors and the wider market only act on financial proof — so the most persuasive way to argue for female leadership is to put a stock-market price on the bias against it.
inferred.one_liner: The first stock index that prices the bias against women leaders.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Converts gender-bias research into a tradable Bloomberg index that outperforms the S&P 500 by 80% over 4 years, attracting 14.5k new EU investors and pushing other investment platforms to host Pink Chip in additional markets.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial board built around a stylised financial chart with a glowing pink Pink Chip line overtaking the male-led and S&P 500 benchmarks, set over a ghosted greyscale portrait of a female leader; left column carries Context/Idea/Execution/Impact text, right column stacks press quotes and a Bloomberg ticker.

idea.rationale: Building a live stock index of women-led companies — Pink Chip — translates the gender-leadership debate into the only language capital markets accept: ticker performance. Listing it on Bloomberg under PINKCHIP:IND turns the argument into a tradable instrument, not a poster, which is a genuinely category-redefining move for purpose work.

strategy.rationale: DEGIRO is a European discount-broker positioning itself around democratising investing, partnered here with UN Women Nederland whose Sustainable Development Goal 5.5 explicitly targets women's leadership in economic life — so a financial-product activism is direct mission-fit for both signatories. The stated context — 7% of listed CEOs are women and biased investors trigger a 2-3% share-price drop on appointment — is sourced to published academic research and the board names an advisory board of academics across five countries, giving the insight stronger backing than most purpose work. The structural risk is that the brand authority sits with UN Women more than DEGIRO, but the broker's role as the distribution platform keeps the partnership coherent.

execution.rationale: AI plus name-recognition software was used to trawl corporate filings and assemble the first systematic data-set of women-led companies, then surfaced as a free site (PinkChip.org), a DEGIRO platform integration and a Bloomberg ticker — three different access routes for retail, professional and institutional audiences. The academic advisory board across five countries supplies methodological credibility, and the orchestration extends from data scrape to index to investable instrument without leaving a step to PR alone.

impact.rationale: The headline business outcome is industry-practice change: 14.5k new EU investors into Pink Chip companies in three weeks and other investment platforms picking up the index in additional markets are tangible behaviour-shift signals for a brokerage. The +182.53% index performance vs +88.43% S&P 500 over four years is an anchored comparison because both series share the same time base, which is rare for purpose-work metrics. The 85M reach and 58M impressions are entrant-reported and treated as supporting rather than load-bearing; the missing piece is a hard DEGIRO commercial outcome (new-account lift attributable to Pink Chip), which keeps it Gold rather than GP.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because it forces gender-bias into the financial system's own dashboard — a live, tradable Bloomberg index that outperforms the S&P 500 — rather than appealing to capital markets from the outside.

campaign: Project Farm Equal — Redesigning an age-old industry with Gender Ergonomics
one_liner: Re-engineer farm tools for the women who now hold them.
search_doc:
Re-engineer farm tools for the women who now hold them.
Mechanisms: industry-practice-change, purpose-driven-product, affected-community-as-co-author, stereotype-flipped-defensibly
Idea: Lay's started Project Farm Equal by re-engineering farm machinery with NIFD (National Institute of Fashion Design) to suit the ergonomic requirements of women. They modified several small-standard hand tools that haven't been changed for centuries — to enable thousands of women in farms to reach their truest potential.
Insight: Indian farm tools were designed for male bodies — so as agriculture is increasingly carried by women, the very hardware of the industry quietly holds them back.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/SILVER/Project Farm Equal - Lay_s (Leo Burnett Mumbai).jpeg

extracted.campaign: Project Farm Equal — Redesigning an age-old industry with Gender Ergonomics
extracted.tagline: Sowing Seeds of Change: The CSR Journal — Empowered Women Cultivate a New Path in Farming.
extracted.context: Farming in India has traditionally been a male-centric occupation. However, in recent times, with more men moving to cities, more women are taking the farms along tools and practices that were traditionally designed for men. This is a significant factor leading to a 30% yield drop in women-led farms.
extracted.idea: Lay's started Project Farm Equal by re-engineering farm machinery with NIFD (National Institute of Fashion Design) to suit the ergonomic requirements of women. They modified several small-standard hand tools that haven't been changed for centuries — to enable thousands of women in farms to reach their truest potential.
execution: Partnered with NIFD to redesign hand tools with gender-ergonomic specifications based on women farmers' measurements
Modified weeders, harvesters and other small farm implements
Distributed redesigned tools to women farmers in West Bengal
Documentary content and editorial press push around the rollout
metrics: 87.6% more women recruited in UP and West Bengal
16% increase in Lay's crop potato production

inferred.insight: Indian farm tools were designed for male bodies — so as agriculture is increasingly carried by women, the very hardware of the industry quietly holds them back.
inferred.one_liner: Re-engineer farm tools for the women who now hold them.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Rewires a centuries-old hand-tool design standard around female ergonomics, lifting Lay's potato yields 16% while recruiting 87.6% more women farmers in UP and West Bengal.

visual.style_description: Wide documentary portrait of an Indian woman and man standing in a field with their height and tool measurements annotated above their heads; right side shows a strip of mocked-up redesigned hand tools; bottom band carries yellow callouts on press logos and metric blocks.

idea.rationale: Redesigning centuries-old Indian farm hand tools around female ergonomics — rather than running a campaign about women farmers — names a structural cause of the 30% yield gap and fixes it at the hardware layer. 'Gender ergonomics' as the category for the intervention is a memorable, ownable frame that travels beyond the launch market.

strategy.rationale: Lay's is PepsiCo's potato-chip brand whose 'Farmer Connect' programme contracts directly with Indian potato farmers for its supply chain, so re-engineering tools for the women now running those farms is on-mission supply-chain stewardship rather than borrowed purpose. The 30% yield drop on women-led farms is a concrete agronomic claim and the partnership with NIFD (National Institute of Fashion Design) for ergonomic measurement supplies methodological credibility, with women farmers measured as the design basis — which puts the affected community closer to co-author than to subject. The board frames the context tightly but does not cite the source for the 30% figure.

execution.rationale: The redesign covers several small standard hand-tools (weeders, harvesters, etc.) measured against women farmers' anthropometrics, distributed to women farmers in West Bengal and Uttar Pradesh through Lay's existing contract-farming network — a real production-and-distribution pipeline, not a prototype film. Documentary content and an editorial press push across The CSR Journal, HERSTORY, The Print and The Week extends the rollout into the agribusiness and women-empowerment news streams relevant to the cause.

impact.rationale: The 16% increase in Lay's crop potato production is an anchored supply-chain business outcome — the right metric for a contract-farming intervention — and 87.6% more women recruited in UP and West Bengal is a behaviour-change number with a regional base. The '43% potential rise in GDP' is a hypothetical national-economy projection rather than a measured outcome, and should not carry weight. Press coverage is broad but reach figures are not disclosed, so the impact case rests credibly on yield and recruitment rather than on amplification claims.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because it rebuilds the physical tools women farmers use rather than running an awareness campaign about them, with anchored yield and recruitment outcomes — but capped below Gold by a thin national-policy or industry-standard footprint.

one_liner: Gender equality in a 250 ml bottle.
search_doc:
Gender equality in a 250 ml bottle.
Mechanisms: purpose-driven-product, affected-community-as-co-author, category-first-formulation, product-as-policy-substitute
Idea: Vaseline launched the world's first clinically proven skincare product for transgender women — the Transition Body Lotion — created with and for the community, addressing skin issues during gender-affirming therapy with patented Unilever technology including isoflavones, a plant-based oestrogen never before used in the category.
Insight: Real change for transgender women doesn't have to come from politics — it can come in the form of a product designed for their actual biological needs.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/GRAND PRIX/Transition Body Lotion - Unilever (Ogilvy Singapore).jpeg

extracted.tagline: The world's first clinically proven skincare product for transgender women — Gender Equality in a 250 ml Bottle.
extracted.context: Thailand's commendable social inclusion of transgender women is visible at all levels of society, but they still face significant issues. One is the transitioning process: hormonal imbalance during this period makes skin three times more prone to dryness, dullness, and hyperpigmentation. Yet no skincare product had ever been formulated specifically for transgender women.
extracted.idea: Vaseline launched the world's first clinically proven skincare product for transgender women — the Transition Body Lotion — created with and for the community, addressing skin issues during gender-affirming therapy with patented Unilever technology including isoflavones, a plant-based oestrogen never before used in the category.
execution: Vaseline Pro Derma Transition Body Lotion formulated for delicate, dry and dull skin
Formulation includes Gluta-Ceramides, 3% Niacinamide, 1% 4D Hyaluronic Acid and Isoflavones
Distribution at 670 Watsons stores across Thailand and online
Endorsement from transgender influencer/businesswoman Nisamanee Nutt
Earned editorial coverage in fashion and lifestyle press
metrics: Available at 670 Watsons stores and online

inferred.insight: Real change for transgender women doesn't have to come from politics — it can come in the form of a product designed for their actual biological needs.
inferred.one_liner: Gender equality in a 250 ml bottle.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns a mass-market skincare brand into an inclusion vehicle for transgender women in Thailand — a clinically proven category-first product backed by 725% purchase intent, 100% positive sentiment, 158M impressions and national retail distribution.

visual.style_description: Hero packshot of the lilac Vaseline Pro Derma Transition Body Lotion bottle centred against a soft lavender-to-pink gradient, flanked by a left column of problem/solution copy and a right column of press logos and headline metrics; calm cosmetics-ad composition rather than a busy case board.

idea.rationale: Reformulating Vaseline's mass-market body lotion as the world's first clinically proven skincare product for transgender women in transition reframes inclusion as a category-first SKU rather than a campaign. The 'gender equality in a 250 ml bottle' line crystallises the conceit: tangible, ownable, and unprecedented in a beauty category that has historically defaulted to cis-female biology.

strategy.rationale: Vaseline is Unilever's century-old global skin-protection brand whose 'See My Skin' / dermatological-equity positioning has been pushing inclusion in skincare for years, so a transgender-specific formulation is on-mission rather than borrowed purpose. The transgender community is treated as co-author of the product (co-created and endorsed by Nisamanee Nutt) and the stated biological context — hormonal transition making skin 'three times more prone' to dryness and hyperpigmentation — is medically plausible, though the board cites it without a named clinical source.

execution.rationale: The formulation pairs proprietary Unilever actives (Gluta-Ceramides, 3% Niacinamide, 1% 4D Hyaluronic Acid) with isoflavones — a plant-based oestrogen never before used in the category — and is rolled out at scale through 670 Watsons stores and online, not as a limited stunt SKU. Influencer endorsement from Nisamanee Nutt and fashion-press editorial in Vogue Thailand, Nylon and Bazaar give the launch credible community-led amplification rather than a paid push.

impact.rationale: National retail distribution across 670 Watsons stores is a durable infrastructure outcome that outlasts the campaign window and represents the real business goal for a CPG launch. The +725% purchase intent is the right consumer-behaviour proxy, but the absolute base is undisclosed — '725% of what baseline?' is not answered on the board. The 158M impressions and 100% positive sentiment are entrant-reported and read as supportive rather than load-bearing; the headline outcome that justifies the GP is the existence and shelf-availability of a category-first product for a community that previously had none.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins the Grand Prix because it converts Glass purpose into a clinically formulated, nationally distributed product co-built with the transgender community, rather than a campaign about them.

one_liner: Lobby congress in the WhatsApp group they cannot leave.
search_doc:
Lobby congress in the WhatsApp group they cannot leave.
Mechanisms: platform-jailbreak, direct-mail-confrontation, legislative-or-policy-shift, victim-as-author
Idea: We invited Costa Rican congresspersons into a WhatsApp group named 'Thank you Congresspersons'. Although we conducted a 360° multimedia campaign to capture the attention of the Costa Rican congresspersons, they ignored us. They focus was elsewhere — their cell phones. In this group, Costa Rican girls tagged them all and thanked them, name by name, in advance, for approving a law that would forever change their future.
Insight: Costa Rican lawmakers ignored every traditional campaign channel, but they couldn't ignore their own phones — so the most powerful place to lobby them was the WhatsApp group they could not mute.

full_record.file_path: 2024/GLASS - THE LION FOR CHANGE/BRONZE/Welcome to the group - Cambiemos La regla (PHD San Jose_TBWA Costarica).jpeg

extracted.tagline: We invited the Costa Rican government to turn a menstrual justice bill into a law.
extracted.context: In Costa Rica, thousands of girls miss school days because they are on their period because they lack the economic resources to buy menstrual products. Nosotras Women Connecting, an NGO that promotes women's rights, proposed a bill that aimed to provide these products for free in schools. We had to convince the Costa Rican congress to approve a law that wasn't even on their radar.
extracted.idea: We invited Costa Rican congresspersons into a WhatsApp group named 'Thank you Congresspersons'. Although we conducted a 360° multimedia campaign to capture the attention of the Costa Rican congresspersons, they ignored us. They focus was elsewhere — their cell phones. In this group, Costa Rican girls tagged them all and thanked them, name by name, in advance, for approving a law that would forever change their future.
execution: Multimedia campaign across channels to draw congress attention (largely ignored)
Created a WhatsApp group named 'Gracias Diputadas/os' and added Costa Rican congresspersons
Girls posted thank-you messages naming each congressperson individually
Video messages embedded in the group amplified the asks
press_coverage: The Congress of Costa Rica unanimously approved the Menstrual Justice Law Project — CNN

inferred.insight: Costa Rican lawmakers ignored every traditional campaign channel, but they couldn't ignore their own phones — so the most powerful place to lobby them was the WhatsApp group they could not mute.
inferred.one_liner: Lobby congress in the WhatsApp group they cannot leave.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Bypasses ignored ad channels by lobbying Costa Rican congresspersons inside a WhatsApp group — five days later the bill was voted through and the Menstrual Justice Law was unanimously approved.

visual.style_description: Dark Congress-chamber background framing a large central smartphone mockup of the 'Gracias Diputadas/os' WhatsApp group with girls' thank-you messages and a video call from a young teenager; left and right columns hold finding/idea text and reportage photos of protests with 'Derechos Menstruales' placards.

idea.rationale: Adding Costa Rican congresspersons to a WhatsApp group named 'Gracias Diputadas/os' where girls thank them by name, in advance, for passing a menstrual-justice bill turns lobbying into an inescapable personal notification. The platform-jailbreak is logically tied to the problem — lawmakers ignored every traditional channel but couldn't ignore their own phones — and the gratitude framing flips the usual protest tone.

strategy.rationale: Nosotras Women Connecting is a Costa Rican NGO promoting women's rights that authored the menstrual-justice bill itself, so the campaign is direct mission-execution rather than borrowed activism. The stated insight — Costa Rican girls miss school because they cannot afford menstrual products — is plausible and the bill was already drafted and tabled, so the policy context is real. The affected girls appear as named authors of the thank-you messages, which satisfies the Glass community-as-co-author test, though the board does not cite the prevalence data behind the school-absence claim.

execution.rationale: After a 360° multimedia campaign failed to land, the work pivots to WhatsApp as the lobbying channel of last resort, with girls tagging each congressperson individually inside the 'Gracias Diputadas/os' group and embedding video messages in the chat. The execution is sharp at the contact-point but thin on documented amplification layers — no coalition of NGOs, no broader press loop visible on the board beyond a single CNN line — so the work rests almost entirely on the single WhatsApp tactic.

impact.rationale: The Congress of Costa Rica's unanimous approval of the Menstrual Justice Law — cited via a CNN headline on the board — is the right and rare business outcome for a legislative campaign and would normally justify a higher tier. However, the board carries no metrics block at all: no read receipts or response rates inside the group, no public-opinion data, no reach figures for the surrounding 360° push. The causal chain from WhatsApp to unanimous vote is therefore narratively credible but evidentially thin, and the surrounding amplification metrics that would let a jury verify the path are absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the WhatsApp jailbreak is on-brief and a unanimous menstrual-justice vote is cited, but the impact case offers a press headline without supporting metrics or evidence that the group itself drove the swing.
